questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a 4-month history of headaches, blurred vision, and pruritus after hot baths. On examination, he has facial plethora and a palpable spleen 3 cm below the costal margin. Blood tests: Hb 18.5 g/dL, haematocrit 0.56, WCC 12.5 × 10^9/L, platelets 650 × 10^9/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Secondary polycythaemia due to chronic hypoxia
questions.explanation: Polycythaemia vera is likely due to the triad of elevated Hb, WCC, and platelets with splenomegaly and pruritus after bathing. Essential thrombocythaemia typically has isolated thrombocytosis. Secondary polycythaemia would have normal WCC and platelets and no splenomegaly. CML has very high WCC with left shift, and myelofibrosis usually has splenomegaly but with anaemia and teardrop cells.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of abdominal pain, distension, and vomiting. She has no past medical history. Examination reveals tender hepatomegaly and ascites. Ultrasound shows hepatic vein thrombosis. Bloods: Hb 16.8 g/dL, haematocrit 0.52, WCC 11.0 × 10^9/L, platelets 500 × 10^9/L, normal O2 saturations. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Budd-Chiari syndrome in a young woman with raised red cell indices and thrombocytosis is a classic thrombotic presentation of polycythaemia vera. PNH also causes thrombosis but typically with haemoglobinuria and low Hb. Antiphospholipid syndrome causes thrombosis but not polycythaemia. Myelofibrosis often presents with splenomegaly but usually anaemia.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old non-smoker presents to the emergency department with acute-onset right-sided weakness and dysarthria. CT head shows an ischaemic stroke. He has no known comorbidities. Bloods: Hb 19.2 g/dL, haematocrit 0.62, WCC 14.0 × 10^9/L, platelets 600 × 10^9/L. Oxygen saturations are 98% on air. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The markedly elevated haematocrit and normal oxygen saturations in a non-smoker make primary polycythaemia vera most likely. Secondary polycythaemia from COPD would have low oxygen saturations. Gaisböck syndrome (relative polycythaemia) occurs with dehydration and normal red cell mass. Essential thrombocythaemia has isolated high platelets, and CML has very high WCC.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known polycythaemia vera, managed with venesection and low-dose aspirin, presents with progressive fatigue, night sweats, and weight loss over 4 months. On examination, his spleen is now palpable 12 cm below the costal margin. Blood film shows teardrop cells, nucleated red cells, and a leucoerythroblastic picture. Hb 8.5 g/dL, platelets 150 × 10^9/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The development of massive splenomegaly, constitutional symptoms, and a leucoerythroblastic blood film with anaemia and thrombocytopenia in a patient with known PV is typical of transformation to myelofibrosis (post-PV MF). AML is less likely without blasts, CML has very high WCC with Philadelphia chromosome, and ET does not evolve into this picture.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman who is otherwise healthy presents with symptoms of pressure headaches and intermittent visual disturbances. She is on a combined oral contraceptive pill. Blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg. Bloods: Hb 16.5 g/dL, haematocrit 0.50, WCC 9.5 × 10^9/L, platelets 450 × 10^9/L. Serum erythropoietin is low. JAK2 V617F mutation is detected. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Secondary polycythaemia from oral contraceptive pill
questions.explanation: Low erythropoietin and positive JAK2 mutation confirm polycythaemia vera even in a young woman on OCP. OCP does not cause polycythaemia; smoking might but not with low EPO. Gaisböck syndrome is relative with normal red cell mass. Essential thrombocythaemia would have higher platelets relative to Hb.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old gentleman presents with an incidental finding of haemoglobin 18.5 g/dL and haematocrit 0.54 on routine bloods. He is asymptomatic with no history of smoking or chronic lung disease. What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
options: Sleep study to exclude sleep apnoea
questions.explanation: JAK2 V617F mutation is present in ~95% of polycythaemia vera and is the first-line diagnostic test per NICE guidelines. A low EPO is supportive but not confirmatory; bone marrow biopsy is not required initially if JAK2 positive. Sleep study and chest X-ray are for secondary causes, which are less likely given no symptoms.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman is found to have haemoglobin 17.0 g/dL and haematocrit 0.51. JAK2 V617F mutation is negative, but serum erythropoietin is low. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Low EPO in the presence of raised Hb is a hallmark of polycythaemia vera, even in the ~5% of JAK2-negative cases (often with CALR mutations). Secondary polycythaemia typically has normal or high EPO; relative polycythaemia has normal EPO and normal red cell mass; ET and CML have thrombocytosis/leucocytosis as primary features.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with confirmed JAK2-positive polycythaemia vera (haemoglobin 18.8 g/dL) is being evaluated. According to WHO diagnostic criteria, when is a bone marrow biopsy mandatory?
options: It is indicated to exclude transformation in sudden splenomegaly
It is not needed for diagnosis if JAK2 positive and Hb/HCT are elevated
It is indicated if the JAK2 mutation is negative
It is always required to confirm the diagnosis
It is recommended for all patients to assess fibrosis at baseline
questions.explanation: In known JAK2-positive PV, a bone marrow biopsy is not required for diagnosis (rule E). It is indicated when there is a sudden change in clinical features (e.g., splenomegaly, leucocytosis) to rule out transformation to myelofibrosis or acute leukaemia. Option A is false; option B is for JAK2-negative cases; option D is not standard.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known polycythaemia vera (JAK2 positive, stable on phlebotomy) presents with rapid onset of massive splenomegaly and leucocytosis (white cell count 45 × 10⁹/L). What is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Sudden splenomegaly and leucocytosis suggest transformation to myelofibrosis or acute leukaemia. Bone marrow biopsy is the gold standard to confirm transformation (e.g., by showing fibrosis or blast cells). JAK2 remains positive; blood film may show abnormalities but is not definitive; EPO and LDH are non-specific.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman who smokes (20/day) has a haemoglobin of 16.5 g/dL and haematocrit 0.48. JAK2 V617F mutation is negative and serum erythropoietin is normal. What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: Given her smoking history, a secondary polycythaemia due to carbon monoxide exposure is likely. Arterial blood gases with carboxyhaemoglobin measurement can identify elevated COHb (>2% in smokers) suggesting smoker's polycythaemia. Chest X-ray and oximetry are less direct; EPO is normal making renal tumour unlikely; bone marrow biopsy is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with polycythaemia vera (JAK2 V617F positive) is asymptomatic and has no history of thrombosis or cardiovascular risk factors. Her haematocrit is 0.52. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her condition?
questions.explanation: For low-risk PV (age <60, no prior thrombosis), venesection (phlebotomy) is first-line to maintain haematocrit <0.45. Hydroxycarbamide is reserved for high-risk patients or those with symptomatic splenomegaly or refractory disease.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with polycythaemia vera and a history of transient ischaemic attack 2 years ago presents with a haematocrit of 0.48. He has no current symptoms. What is the most appropriate first-line cytoreductive therapy?
questions.explanation: In high-risk PV (age >60 or prior thrombosis), hydroxycarbamide is first-line cytoreductive therapy. Venesection alone is insufficient; ruxolitinib is second-line for hydroxycarbamide resistance or intolerance. Anagrelide is used mainly for essential thrombocythaemia.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with polycythaemia vera on long-term hydroxycarbamide develops a painful leg ulcer that fails to heal after 4 weeks of conservative management. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue hydroxycarbamide and add topical steroids
questions.explanation: Hydroxycarbamide can cause leg ulcers; switching to interferon alfa is appropriate as it is an alternative cytoreductive agent without this side effect. Anagrelide is not first-line for PV; ruxolitinib is reserved for refractory cases. Increasing dose or continuing would worsen the ulcer.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with polycythaemia vera on aspirin and venesection develops an acute ischaemic stroke. Her haematocrit is 0.52. She is not a candidate for thrombolysis. What is the most important additional acute treatment for her polycythaemia vera?
options: Urgent venesection to reduce haematocrit below 0.45
questions.explanation: In acute thrombosis with PV, urgent venesection is indicated to rapidly lower haematocrit (target <0.45) and reduce blood viscosity. Hydroxycarbamide is used for long-term control, not acute. Clopidogrel is not first-line over aspirin in this context.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with polycythaemia vera is planning pregnancy. She is currently controlled with venesection alone and has no prior thrombosis. Which cytoreductive agent is considered safe to continue or use during pregnancy if needed?
options: No cytoreductive agent is safe; continue venesection only
questions.explanation: Interferon alfa is safe in pregnancy and is the preferred cytoreductive agent if required. Hydroxycarbamide, anagrelide, and ruxolitinib are teratogenic or lack safety data. Venesection is first-line but if cytoreduction is needed, interferon alfa is the correct choice.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of headache, pruritus after hot baths, and a ruddy complexion. Observations: BP 145/85, HR 80, saturations 98%. Bloods: Hb 185 g/L, haematocrit 0.56, platelets 600 × 10^9/L, WBC 12 × 10^9/L. He is not acutely unwell. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Request bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy
questions.explanation: In suspected polycythaemia vera, the first step is to confirm the diagnosis with JAK2 mutation testing, which is positive in >95% of cases. Initiating treatment (venesection, aspirin, or cytoreduction) without a confirmed diagnosis is premature and could be inappropriate if the polycythaemia has another cause.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known JAK2-positive polycythaemia vera attends clinic. She has been managed with venesection every 2–3 months to maintain haematocrit <0.45, and takes aspirin 75 mg daily. She feels well. Current bloods: Hb 150 g/L, haematocrit 0.44, platelets 500 × 10^9/L, WBC 10 × 10^9/L. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: The patient is well-controlled with haematocrit <0.45 on venesection and aspirin. The platelet count of 500 × 10^9/L alone, without symptoms or high-risk features, is not an indication for starting cytoreduction. Continuing current management is appropriate.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known polycythaemia vera (on hydroxycarbamide and aspirin) is brought to the emergency department with sudden severe headache, vomiting, and right-sided weakness. On examination: GCS 13, BP 160/90, HR 55, irregular pulse. CT head shows a large left-sided intracerebral haemorrhage. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start therapeutic apheresis to rapidly lower haematocrit
Give desmopressin (DDAVP) to improve platelet function
Reverse anticoagulation (if on warfarin) with vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate
Start low molecular weight heparin for thromboprophylaxis
questions.explanation: In a patient with acute intracerebral haemorrhage, the priority is neurosurgical evaluation for possible surgical intervention. While haematological optimisation (e.g., reversal of anticoagulation) is important, calling neurosurgery is the immediate next step to assess the need for decompression or clot evacuation.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with polycythaemia vera (on venesection and aspirin) presents with acute severe left upper quadrant pain radiating to the left shoulder, and fever (38.5°C). Observations: BP 100/60, HR 110, saturations 95%. Abdominal examination reveals tender splenomegaly. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Request a CT angiogram of the abdomen
Call the surgical registrar for an emergency laparotomy
questions.explanation: In polycythaemia vera, acute left upper quadrant pain with fever and tender splenomegaly is suggestive of splenic infarction. Urgent abdominal ultrasound is the quickest investigation to confirm or exclude this. Starting antibiotics without a clear infective source or performing interventions like venesection are not the first priority.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with JAK2-positive polycythaemia vera is currently 16 weeks pregnant. She is asymptomatic and has not previously been on cytoreductive therapy. Current bloods: Hb 170 g/L, haematocrit 0.50, platelets 700 × 10^9/L. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform urgent venesection to reduce haematocrit to <0.45
Refer to obstetric haematology for shared care
Start low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg once daily)
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, low-dose aspirin is recommended for all patients with polycythaemia vera to reduce thrombotic risk. Hydroxycarbamide is teratogenic and contraindicated. Venesection may be considered if haematocrit remains elevated despite aspirin, but aspirin is the first step. Referral to obstetric haematology is appropriate but the immediate action is starting aspirin.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known polycythaemia vera presents to the emergency department with a sudden severe headache, visual disturbance, and confusion. He has not been on regular venesection recently. What clinical finding on examination would most strongly indicate an emergency requiring immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: Papilloedema indicates retinal vein engorgement from hyperviscosity syndrome, a medical emergency that can lead to retinal vein occlusion and stroke. Hypertension is common but not the specific danger; splenomegaly and plethora are typical of PV but not acute; gout is a late complication.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with polycythaemia vera attends for routine venesection. She is asymptomatic, and her blood count today shows: Hb 150 g/L, Hct 0.42, WCC 11.2 × 10⁹/L, platelets 1150 × 10⁹/L, ferritin 30 μg/L. Which single result is a red flag requiring escalation of therapy?
questions.explanation: A platelet count consistently above 1000 × 10⁹/L increases thrombotic risk and is an indication for cytoreductive therapy (e.g. hydroxyurea). The haematocrit is within target (<0.45), WCC is only mildly elevated, and ferritin is low but expected after venesection.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with polycythaemia vera presents with acute-onset shortness of breath and pleuritic chest pain. He appears anxious and is sweating. Which examination finding most strongly suggests a life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Elevated JVP indicates right ventricular strain, a key sign of massive pulmonary embolism. Unilateral calf swelling suggests DVT but not the acute threat; pleural rub is non-specific; tachycardia alone is insufficient; facies is chronic.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with polycythaemia vera treated with venesection and hydroxyurea presents with new-onset fever, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss over 4 weeks. On examination, what finding is most concerning for transformation to myelofibrosis or acute leukaemia?
options: Hepatomegaly 2 cm below the costal margin
Splenomegaly 4 cm below the costal margin
questions.explanation: Constitutional symptoms (fever, night sweats) along with new lymphadenopathy suggest extramedullary haematopoiesis or transformation. Splenomegaly is common in PV, but new significant lymphadenopathy is more specific for myelofibrosis or leukaemic transformation.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with polycythaemia vera on hydroxyurea and low-dose aspirin for 2 years presents with a 3-day history of fever, sore throat, and malaise. On examination, she has a temperature of 38.5 °C. Which finding should prompt immediate investigation for a life-threatening drug side-effect?
questions.explanation: Painful oral ulcers (mucositis) in the setting of HAART and fever suggest hydroxyurea-induced neutropenia and risk of sepsis. Petechiae indicate thrombocytopenia; pharyngitis alone is not specific; lymphadenopathy could be reactive; joint swelling suggests gout or arthritis.
